caption_from_docs: Fig. 8. shows a sphere of radius a in an infinite conducting medium with a current I transmitting at a voltage V.  Since current is conserved, the current density at a distance r is J = I/4πr2 . If the resistivity of the medium is ρ, then E = ρJ = ρI/ 4πr2.  The voltage V is the integral of E, V = ρI/ 4πa so the resistance to earth is ρ/ 4πa.  A more realistic geometry is the half plane shown by the dotted line (a hemisphere at the surface), which doubles the resistance. A typical clay soil might have a resistivity of 20 ohm m so a 1 m radius sphere, (or flat plate) would have an earth resistance of about 3 ohms, very much less than the cable resistance, but, as discussed below, the situation is not quite so simple.  In sea water it would be a factor 100 lower.

caption_from_docs: Figure 5 shows three small bodies of unit mass, initially in contact, attracted by a mass M.  Their accelerations, starting with the left-most, are MG/(d+a2), MG/d2 and MG/(d-a)2.  After a short time t, they become separated by a distance  L=t2a/d3 and, to first order, the bodies are still symmetric about the centre mass.  The Schumacher Levy comet approaching Jupiter was pulled apart as a result of these forces.  Fortunately, our moon is less disruptive and just produces a symmetric bulge, which is static because of the added orbital velocity.

caption_from_docs: Figure 2 shows the vapour pressures of a large number of elements as a function of temperature.  Since the vapour is thermally activated from the solid, or liquid, to the gas phase we expect the vapour pressure to be proportional to exp(-U/kT) where U is the activation energy, k is Boltzman’s constant and T the absolute temperature.  From the above, therefore, we expect U to be a few electron volts.
caption_in_spreadsheet: Fig. 7.2. The structure of an alkali metal.

caption_from_docs: Figure 3 is an Arrhenius plot of log pressure against 1/T, which should be a straight line whose gradient gives the activation energy.  This works very well. It can be seen that, although the vapour pressures vary by ten orders of magnitudes, the gradients vary by only a factor of ten.  For carbon U = 8  eV and for mercury U = 0.5eV with all the other elements between them.  We see that our argument that all atomic binding energies are, within an order of magnitude, a few electron volts is borne out.
caption_in_spreadsheet: Fig. 7.3. log vapour pressure plotted against 1/T.

caption_from_docs: Fig. 3.  The projection of the circles from satellites A (orange) and B (blue) at two times T1 (solid) and T2 (dashed).  The signal at T1 alone fixes the receiver at either R1 or R1’. An additional satellite fix is required to remove the ambiguity. Alternatively, the signal at T2 can be used provided that the receiver is known not to have moved any appreciable distance. In choosing between R2 and R2’, R2 makes better sense.

caption_from_docs: Figure 10 shows the old Cheddar’s Lane sewage pumping station in Cambridge.  This is now a museum with the original steam pumps in working order.  Also of interest is that these ran almost entirely on waste.  This mostly took the form of the ash from the coal fires that heated every house in the city.  Although apparently burnt to a cinder, in fact an open fire only uses about half the energy in the coal and the much higher temperatures in the furnace of the station meant the other 50% could be used to run the pumps.  For the purposes of this chapter, we estimate the velocity of the draught this 180-feet high chimney can generate.

caption_from_docs: Figure 15 shows the flow round a cylinder (it’s easier to analyse a cylinder than a sphere) that is spinning in the clockwise direction. The cylinder is moving from right to left, but we get the same effect if the cylinder is stationary in a flow going from left to right. The velocity on the top surface is increased and that on the bottom decreased.  From Bernoulli’s equation the pressure is less on the top than the bottom so there is a vertical force on the cylinder.  This is consistent with the fact that the centrifugal pressure gradient is increased on the top since the radius of curvature of the streamlines remains close to that of the cylinder, while the velocity is increased by the spinning cylinder. The flow depicted in Fig 15 is “back spin” and the lift created causes the ball to stay in the air longer. Conversely, spin in the opposite direction is called “top spin” which pulls the ball down to the ground faster. This is extremely beneficial in tennis, as the ball can be very fast with top spin without overshooting the baseline and backspin in the Dambusters raid meant that the spinning bombs hit the surface of the water gently enabling them to bounce without blowing up.

caption_from_docs: Figure 11 shows how the flattening of the earth at the poles causes a bulge around the equator, due to centrifugal force.  The resulting shape is called an oblate spheroid. caption_from_docs: Figure 4 shows the Earth with its spin axis vertical so that the equatorial plane is horizontal. For ease of analysis, the Sun is shown orbiting the earth in the plane of the ecliptic, which is inclined at inclined at the angle of θ = 23.5°. From the point of view of the Earth, and ignoring the Earth’s rotation, the Sun orbits once a year. What we see on a sundial is affected by the apparent angular speed of the Sun projected onto a horizontal plane. The analysis gets a bit hairy but ultimately, we find “the equation of time”.
